elements.sent-2411-004: Following exposure to intense noxious stimuli, a sensitization process occurs to adapt the dorsal horn functional state.
elements.sent-2411-005: Notably, projection neurons can display a switch in firing pattern from tonic firing to plateau potentials with sustained afterdischarges.
elements.sent-2411-006: For afterdischarges to manifest after this switch, the neuron must develop bistability, which characterizes the ability to show resting or spiking at the same input current depending on the context.
elements.sent-2411-007: In numerous instances, neuronal bistability arises through voltage-gated calcium channels.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, computational studies have demonstrated a trade-off between bistability and the plausibility of its resting states if calcium channels are counterbalanced with voltage-gated potassium channels.
elements.sent-2411-009: Current knowledge leaves a gap in understanding the underlying mechanisms by which robust bistability, plateau potentials, and sustained afterdischarges emerge in neurons via calcium channels.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this study, we used a conductance-based model to explore the mechanisms by which L-type calcium (CaL) channels can achieve bistability when combined with either M-type potassium (KM) channels or inward-rectifying potassium (Kir) channels.
elements.sent-2411-011: Unlike KM channels, Kir channels enhance bistability.
elements.sent-2411-012: Combined with CaL channels, KM and Kir channels promote different types of bistability, with distinct robustness, and function.
elements.sent-2411-013: An analysis of their inward/outward properties revealed that their distinct steady-state currents explain this contrast.
elements.sent-2411-014: Altogether, the complementary of CaL and Kir channels creates a reliable possible pathway for central sensitization in the dorsal horn.
elements.sent-2411-015: Neuronal bistability underlies short-term memory in neurons associated with sleep rhythms, motor control, working memory, epilepsy, schizophrenia, or pain amplification.
elements.sent-2411-016: At the single-cell level, bistability may emerge through the upregulation of voltage-gated calcium channels.
elements.sent-2411-017: Still, this phenomenon requires additional channels, such as voltage-gated potassium channels, to manifest within physiological ranges.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this context, however, a trade-off exists between bistability and the plausibility of its resting potentials, making it unclear how robust bistability appears physiologically.
elements.sent-2411-019: Using a conductance-based model, we explored how M-type or inward rectifier potassium channels combined with L-type calcium channels influence bistability.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our findings demonstrate that bistability may rely on ion channels interactions that bypass the aforementioned trade-off and promote distinct features relevant to neuronal processing.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the event of a significant increase in threat intensity, sensitization triggers functional changes within the nociceptive system (\cite{latremoliere_central_2009 ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: These changes facilitate the triggering of pain and promote the subsequent recovery process.
elements.sent-2411-023: Dorsal horn projection neurons are a critical relay for sensory input to be transmitted to supraspinal centers during nociception.
elements.sent-2411-024: A subset of mechanisms contributing to sensitization target projection neurons, rendering them hyperexcitable and thus amplifying nociception (\cite{reali_integrated_2005,monteiro_switching-and_2006,zain_alterations_2019 ).}
elements.sent-2411-025: In particular, deep dorsal horn projection neurons exhibit an excitability switch under a change in modulatory inputs.
elements.sent-2411-026: More specifically, projection neurons can switch from tonic firing to plateau potentials with sustained afterdischarges (\cite{derjean_dynamic_2003 ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: These afterdischarges are a sign of hyperexcitability of fundamental importance because they represent a substantial flow of information sent to supraspinal centers, triggering hyperalgesia and indicating a profound change in the sensory signal transmission properties (\cite{cata_altered_2006,monteiro_switching-and_2006,robinson_altered_2014 ).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Indeed, afterdischarges extend the response to noxious stimuli by sustaining spiking following its end rather than reverting to a resting state---a feature commonly designated by bistability (\cite{monteiro_switching-and_2006 ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: Neuronal bistability has been the subject of investigation in several different neuronal populations (\cite{lee_bistability_1998,kazantsev_bistability_2011,dovzhenok_exploring_2012,engbers_bistability_2013,franci_balance_2013,borges_roles_2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, the mechanisms by which robust and physiological bistability emerges in neurons at the single-cell level are not fully understood.
elements.sent-2411-031: Computational studies revealed that bistability between a resting and a spiking state may arise with a targeted increase in voltage-gated calcium channel conductance (\cite{crunelli_window_2005,le_bistable_2006,borges_roles_2023 ), but this must be combined with voltage-gated potassium channels to compensate for the induced hyperpolarization of resting potentials.}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, classical voltage-gated potassium channels also have the side effect of significantly reducing the level of bistability (\cite{yuen_bistability_1995,franci_balance_2013,naudin_general_2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: This leads to a trade-off between bistability robustness and resting potential rectification.
elements.sent-2411-034: Conversely, an increasing body of work showed that the inward rectifier potassium (Kir) channels, a particular type of voltage-gated potassium channels can induce bistability (\cite{shoemaker_neural_2011,sanders_nmda_2013,amarillo_inward_2018,delmoe_conditions_2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-035: Evidence indicated that Kir channels are also present in superficial and deep projection neurons in the dorsal horn (\cite{derjean_dynamic_2003,murata_neuronal_2016,ford_inward-rectifying_2016,malcangio_gabab_2018,brewer_enhanced_2018 ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: Nevertheless, the impact of the interplay between Kir and calcium channels in diverse proportions on bistability remains to be elucidated, as do the underlying mechanisms involved.
elements.sent-2411-037: In light of this, we examined how the level of bistability changes in response to simultaneous increases in the conductance of L-type calcium (CaL) channels and the conductance of either the M-type potassium (KM) channels---a classical type of voltage-gated potassium channels---or the Kir channels, in a neuron with a reduced set of additional ion channels.
elements.sent-2411-038: For this purpose, we used a conductance-based model to determine how these two couples of ion channels affect bistability and reveal the mechanisms involved in each case.
elements.sent-2411-039: This approach demonstrated that, when coupled with CaL channels, Kir channels enhance bistability between resting and spiking, which is not observed with KM channels.
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, our results indicated that these distinct couples of voltage-gated ion channels give rise to two types of bistability that differ in robustness to intrinsic variability and noisy input currents.
elements.sent-2411-041: The contrasted shapes of the Kir and KM steady-state currents justified these differences.
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally, we explored the excitability switches that emerge when CaL channels are combined with either Kir or KM channels, and provided a computational explanation for the distinct robustness observed.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this study, we used a single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model to characterize the excitability of a neuron.
elements.sent-2411-044: This method consists of computing the evolution of the membrane potential $V$ in response to a given applied current $I_\text{app}$ as: \begin{linenomath \begin{equation}C \frac{dV}{dt} = - I_\mathrm{leak} - \sum_{\mathrm{ion} \in \mathcal{I}} I_{\mathrm{ion}} + I_\text{app} ,\end{equation} \end{linenomath where $C$ is the membrane capacitance, $ I_{\mathrm{leak }$ is the leak current, $I_{\mathrm{ion }$ are the intrinsic ionic currents with $\mathcal{I}$ the set of all ionic channels.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: In this work, only voltage-dependent ion channels are considered, and each ionic current is defined by: \begin{linenomath \begin{equation}I_{\mathrm{ion}} = \bar{g}_\mathrm{ion} \, m_{\mathrm{ion}}^{p_{\mathrm{ion}}} \, h_{\mathrm{ion}}^{q_{\mathrm{ion}}} \, (V-E_{\mathrm{ion}}) ,\end{equation} \end{linenomath where $\bar{g _{\mathrm{ion }$ denotes the ion channels maximal conductance, $m_{\mathrm{ion }$ and $h_{\mathrm{ion }$ correspond to the voltage-dependent activation and inactivation gates of the ion channels, $p_{\mathrm{ion }$ is an integer between $1$ and $4$, $q_{\mathrm{ion }$ is an integer between $0$ and $1$, and $E_{\mathrm{ion }$ denotes the Nernst potential of the considered ionic current.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The evolutions of the activation and inactivation gates of a given ion channel are defined as: \begin{linenomath \begin{equation}\frac{dm_{ion}}{dt} = \left(m_{ion,\infty}(V)-m_{ion} \right)/ \tau_{m_{ion}}(V) , \frac{dh_{ion}}{dt} = \left(h_{ion,\infty}(V)-h_{ion} \right)/ \tau_{h_{ion}}(V) ,\end{equation} \end{linenomath where $m_{\mathrm{ion ,\infty (V)$ and $h_{\mathrm{ion ,\infty (V)$ denote the steady-state values of the activation and inactivation gates at a given membrane potential $V$, and $\tau_{m_{\mathrm{ion (V)$ and $\tau_{h_{\mathrm{ion (V)$ the time constants of the activation and inactivation gates at a given membrane potential $V$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The ionic currents considered here consist of a voltage-gated fast sodium current $I_{\text{Na }$, a slow delayed rectifier potassium current $I_{\text{KDR }$, a L-type calcium current $I_{\text{CaL }$, a M-type potassium current $I_{\text{KM }$, an inward rectifier potassium current $I_{\text{Kir }$, and a leak current $I_{\text{leak }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The model of $I_{\text{Na }$ and $I_{\text{KDR }$ was based on \cite{destexhe_model_1994 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-074: A frequency-current (fI) curve represents the steady-state spiking frequency observed at a constant applied current.
elements.sent-2411-075: In the context of a bistable neuron, an overlap exists between a zero-frequency state (\ie, a resting state) and a non-zero-frequency state (\ie, a spiking state) within a range of current values in which bistability is observed.
elements.sent-2411-076: In this work, we designate this range of current the bistability window, which is defined between the onset of spiking at current $I_1$ and the cessation of resting at $I_2$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Thus, the initial conditions used when computing the steady-state spiking frequency for currents within the bistability window must allow the neuron to converge to its spiking state.
elements.sent-2411-078: To determine them, we initialized the neuron at resting and applied a depolarizing current step from the $i^{\text{th }$ current (in the range of current tested) to a higher current, out of the bistability window.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Then, the final state observed was used as the initial conditions to simulate the $i^{\text{th }$ constant current and determine the corresponding steady-state spiking frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This ensures that the ultra-slow inactivation gate of calcium channels is still open, and facilitates the convergence towards spiking, even with low steady-state frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-081: The model is then simulated for windows of \SI{80 {\second until the spiking frequency reaches steady-state.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: To compute the line of zero-frequency of the fI curve, we computed the model fixed points for the same range of current and incorporated a zero-frequency point if a stable fixed point was identified.
elements.sent-2411-083: The combination of these 2 computations was used in Fig.~\ref{111112 C (top) and Figs.~\ref{222222a B and E.}}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Computation of the bistability window heatmaps
elements.sent-2411-090: The computation of the fI curve is associated with a fixed set of conductances.
elements.sent-2411-091: To capture the evolution of the level of bistability, we varied the couple of maximal conductances, $\bar{g _\mathrm{CaL}$ and either $\bar{g _\mathrm{KM}$ or $\bar{g _\mathrm{Kir}$, and computed for each couple of conductances the (absolute) size of the bistability window, noted: \begin{linenomath \begin{equation}\Delta I = I_2-I_1.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The stable resting states were computed using the same methodology to compute the fI curve zero-frequency line.
elements.sent-2411-093: In contrast to the methodology employed in the computation of the descending fI curve, which determined the steady-state spiking frequency for each current within a specified range, in this instance, only the initial current, $I_1$, situated at the beginning of the bistability window, was subjected to analysis.
elements.sent-2411-094: To compute $I_1$ for a given couple of conductances in a reasonable execution time, this algorithm was modified by employing an ascending range of current and initiated at a current from the resting-only regime.

elements.sent-2411-147: In this work, we used a conductance-based model to contrast the effect of the M-type (KM) and of the inward rectifier (Kir) potassium channels when coupled to L-type calcium (CaL) channels on bistability between resting and spiking states, which was either weakened or strengthened (respectively).
elements.sent-2411-148: Then, we point out the relevance of a couple of CaL and Kir channels for bistability by quantifying their robustness to noisy input currents and intrinsic variability using both types of potassium channels (KM or Kir) in combination with CaL channels.
elements.sent-2411-149: Next, we investigate how the features of the steady-state currents of CaL, KM, and Kir channels shape the ability to reinforce bistability and identify a region of negative differential conductance in the Kir current around the spike threshold, similar to that of the CaL current.
elements.sent-2411-150: To demonstrate that this feature is pivotal for strengthening bistability, we show that the outward KM and Kir currents weaken and strengthen bistability respectively.
elements.sent-2411-151: In contrast, the inward KM and Kir currents do not interfere with bistability.
elements.sent-2411-152: Finally, we analyze the excitability switches created by CaL channels when coupled with either KM or Kir channels and relate these switches to bistability and plateau-type excitability.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: In combination with CaL channels, KM channels weaken bistability, while Kir channels strengthen it.
elements.sent-2411-153: When combined with fast voltage-gated sodium channels and slow delayed-rectifier potassium channels, voltage-gated calcium channels create bistability between resting and spiking, but at unrealistically low resting potentials.
elements.sent-2411-154: This effect is thought to be counterbalanced when calcium channels are coupled with voltage-gated potassium channels.
elements.sent-2411-155: However, common voltage-gated potassium channels do not solely rectify those equilibria, but weaken bistability.
elements.sent-2411-156: In this section, we illustrate this problem, first by adding L-type calcium (CaL) channels to a conductance-based model, creating unphysiological bistability, and then by adding M-type potassium (KM) channels, rectifying resting potentials at the cost of weakening bistability.
elements.sent-2411-157: Finally, we show that when adding instead a particular type of voltage-gated potassium channels, the inward rectifier potassium (Kir) channels, this trade-off does not exist, suggesting that those channels promote robust and physiological bistability when combined with calcium channels.. \selectlanguage{english.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=17.6cm]{figures/f1-new-vf.pdf} \caption{{Voltage-gated calcium channels produce bistability--the capacity to display either resting or spiking at a given current value, depending on the initial conditions. A, When initially resting, the application of ascending steps of current with increasing amplitude results in a progressive depolarization of the membrane potential (purple and reds). At high current values, the neuron switches from resting to spiking (yellow). B, When initially spiking, the application of descending steps of current with increasing amplitude results in a progressive decrease of the firing frequency (yellow and reds). At low current values, the neuron switches from spiking to resting (purple). C, The superimposition of the fI curves computed from resting and spiking respectively identifies the bistability window (top), that is, the range of currents from $I_1$ to $I_2$ within which both resting or spiking can be observed, as for A and B, in reds. The resting equilibrium potentials within and below the bistability window are progressively depolarized as the current increases (bottom). {\label{111112}} }} \end{center}

elements.sent-2411-245: The mechanisms by which Kir and KM exert such disparate effects on the bistability window size remain unclear.
elements.sent-2411-246: As both channels are voltage-gated potassium channels and operate in a similar timescale, the main difference is the range of potentials at which they are opened.

elements.sent-2411-248: This section compares the characteristics of the steady-state currents produced by each type of ion channels that alters the onset of physiological bistability.
elements.sent-2411-249: Calcium channels produce an inward current (Fig.~\ref{555555a A, top) that creates bistability.}
elements.sent-2411-250: This current is associated with a region of negative differential conductance near the spike threshold (about \SI{-65 {\milli\volt ) (Fig.~\ref{555555a A, bottom, shaded area).}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Previous research indicated that this property of calcium channels is responsible for the onset of bistability (\cite{franci_balance_2013,drion_dynamic_2015 ).}
elements.sent-2411-252: As for common voltage-gated potassium channels, KM channels exhibit a small inward current and a strong outward current (Fig.~\ref{555555a B, top), that do not show any region of negative differential conductance (Fig.~\ref{555555a B, bottom).}}
elements.sent-2411-253: Unlike classical voltage-gated potassium channels, Kir channels exhibit a strong inward current at potentials below their reversal potential $E_\mathrm{K}$, and a small outward current above it (Fig.~\ref{555555a C, top).}
elements.sent-2411-254: In analogy with the inward current that flows in calcium channels, the steady-state outward current that flows in Kir channels also displays a region of negative differential conductance around and above the spike threshold (Fig.~\ref{555555a C, bottom).}
elements.sent-2411-255: In this range of potentials, a small increase in membrane potential around the spike threshold corresponds to a decrease in outward Kir current, which favors a further increase in membrane potential, and so on, which leads to spiking.
elements.sent-2411-256: Consequently, the sign of the differential conductance around the spike threshold could be an indicator of voltage-gated potassium channels' tendency to reduce or enhance bistability.
elements.sent-2411-257: If this is the case, blocking of the current around the spike threshold, and the induced removal of these regions of negative/positive differential conductance, may result in the removal of the decrease/increase in the bistability window size observed.
elements.sent-2411-258: This hypothesis is the subject of further investigation in the next section.

elements.sent-2411-360: In this study, we used a conductance-based model to investigate how two different couples of ion channels can create plateau potentials and robust bistability.
elements.sent-2411-361: Our results demonstrated that the simultaneous increase in the conductances of L-type calcium (CaL) channels and inward rectifier potassium (Kir) channels enhances both plateau potentials and robust bistability.
elements.sent-2411-362: Unlike other potassium channels, we show that Kir channels promote bistability through their unique steady-state current shape.
elements.sent-2411-363: The cooperation between CaL and Kir channels represents a candidate pathway to enhance projection neuron activity, amplifying nociception during central sensitization.
elements.sent-2411-364: Unlike classical voltage-gated potassium channels, Kir channels strengthen bistability when combined with CaL channels.
elements.sent-2411-365: To understand this effect, we investigated the contributions of Kir channels to bistability by blocking either the outward or the inward Kir current.
elements.sent-2411-366: This analysis revealed that, on the one hand, the outward Kir current enhances the size of the bistability window---the range of currents at which both resting and spiking exist---and shifts it towards higher currents as its maximal conductance increases.
elements.sent-2411-367: On the other hand, the inward Kir current only rectifies the resting equilibrium potentials up to their reversal potential, $E_K$.
elements.sent-2411-368: Therefore, in line with \cite{amarillo_inward_2018 , we support that the ability of Kir channels to enhance bistability must originate from the singular shape of their outward steady-state current, which is markedly distinct from those of classical types of voltage-gated potassium channels.}
elements.sent-2411-369: The same analysis was conducted with M-type potassium (KM) channels, which open at a similar timescale as Kir channels but differ in the shape of their steady-state current.
elements.sent-2411-370: The results showed that the outward KM current is responsible for reducing the size of the bistability window.
elements.sent-2411-371: Additionally, the inward KM current exhibited a rectification of resting equilibrium potentials, although this was less pronounced than that observed with the inward Kir current.
elements.sent-2411-372: These results suggest that the shape of these steady-state outward currents captures their effect on bistability, while both KM and Kir currents increase the current range at which bistability can be observed.
elements.sent-2411-373: Modulatory inputs may alter the range of currents in which bistability exists, which could result in long-lasting afterdischarges to a greater or lesser extent.
elements.sent-2411-374: The occurrence of long-lasting afterdischarges is highly dependent on the input currents and their relative position to the bistability window.
elements.sent-2411-375: Long-lasting afterdischarges appear after applying an input current pulse only if its baseline current is within the range of the bistability window.
elements.sent-2411-376: Therefore, if the baseline and amplitude of a pulse of current are constant, long-lasting afterdischarges may become less frequently observed if the bistability window limits increase (\eg, if the Kir channels conductance increases).
elements.sent-2411-377: In that case, the baseline current may become lower than the limits of the bistability window, which would prevent long-lasting afterdischarges from occurring.
elements.sent-2411-378: Conversely, if modulatory inputs shift the limits of the bistability window to lower values (\eg, if the Kir channels conductance decreases), long-lasting afterdischarges may become more frequently observed.
elements.sent-2411-379: Those results align with the experimental results reported in \cite{derjean_dynamic_2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-380: However, it seems reasonable to assume that long-lasting afterdischarges and persistent firing may eventually cease in vivo.
elements.sent-2411-381: In such a scenario, robust bistability could be combined with additional mechanisms at the single-cell or network level, operating on a slower timescale than Kir channels, that terminate long-lasting afterdischarges.
elements.sent-2411-382: The strong bistability created following the simultaneous increase in both CaL and Kir channels conductances results in the generation of plateau potentials and long-lasting afterdischarges robust to perturbations in input current and intrinsic variability.
elements.sent-2411-383: After applying Normal noise superimposed on the input applied current, the neuron was able to maintain its initial state (resting or spiking) as long as the noisy input current magnitude remained within the limits of the bistability window, which can be of the order of \SI{1 {\micro\ampere / \centi\metre^2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-384: Moreover, this experiment revealed that the resting state can withstand higher perturbations than the spiking state at the center current of the bistability window.
elements.sent-2411-385: This suggests that resting is a functional state that is more robust than spiking when the input current is corrupted by noise.
elements.sent-2411-386: Such behavior seems relevant in pain processing, as the neuron tends to be silent rather than transferring spikes if corrupted by noisy input signals.
elements.sent-2411-387: Furthermore, our results showed that the bistability arising from the combination of CaL and Kir channels is bounded within $\pm$\SI{10 {\percent of their nominal value when exposed to intrinsic variability of \SI{30 {\percent .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-388: This suggests that this type of bistability remains reliable when considering a heterogeneous population of neurons.
elements.sent-2411-389: Therefore,CaL and Kir channels may provide a reliable pathway targeted during central sensitization.
elements.sent-2411-390: In a broader sense, Kir channels may serve as a fundamental component in the establishment of a rudimentary form of memory at the single-cell level, particularly in other functions of the nervous system where the storage of contextual information is of fundamental importance (\cite{sanders_nmda_2013,amarillo_inward_2018,delmoe_conditions_2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-391: This study used a conductance-based model to demonstrate that robust and physiological bistability emerges when L-type calcium channels (CaL) are combined with inward rectifier potassium (Kir) channels.
elements.sent-2411-392: The cooperation between these two types of channels is such that they shift the current range in which bistability is observed (\ie, the bistability window) in opposite directions, but at the same time both strengthen it by increasing its size.
elements.sent-2411-393: We propose that this cooperation relies on a common principle: the existence of a region of negative slope in the steady-state currents around the spike threshold.
elements.sent-2411-394: Evidence supported that the emerging bistability is robust to intrinsic variability and to noise superimposed on the input current.
elements.sent-2411-395: Furthermore, the type of bistability examined in this study is manifested by the appearance of plateau potentials, combined with sustained afterdischarges.
elements.sent-2411-396: The synergic increase in CaL and Kir conductances may represent a potential pathway targeted during central sensitization to switch the functional state of nociception.. \selectlanguage{english \FloatBarrier \bibliographystyle{jneurosci.}}
elements.sent-2411-397: In the dorsal horn, projection neurons play a crucial role in pain processing by transmitting sensory stimuli to supraspinal centers during nociception.
elements.sent-2411-398: Following exposure to intense noxious stimuli, a sensitization process occurs that alters the functional state of the dorsal horn.
elements.sent-2411-399: Notably, projection neurons can undergo a switch in firing pattern---from tonic firing to plateau potentials with sustained afterdischarges.
elements.sent-2411-400: For afterdischarges to occur following this switch, the neuron must exhibit bistability, defined as the ability to exhibit resting and spiking states at the same input current depending on the prior context.
elements.sent-2411-401: In many cases, neuronal bistability arises through the activity of voltage-gated calcium channels.
elements.sent-2411-402: However, computational studies have shown a trade-off between bistability and the plausibility of resting states when calcium channels are counterbalanced by voltage-gated potassium channels.
elements.sent-2411-403: Despite this, the mechanisms underlying the emergence of robust bistability, plateau potentials, and sustained afterdischarges through calcium channels remain poorly understood.
elements.sent-2411-404: In this study, we used a conductance-based model to investigate how L-type calcium (CaL) channels can give rise to bistability when combined with either M-type potassium (KM) channels or inward-rectifying potassium (Kir) channels.
elements.sent-2411-405: When combined with CaL channels, KM and Kir channels promote distinct forms of bistability, differing in both robustness and functional implications.
elements.sent-2411-406: An analysis of their inward and outward current properties revealed that these differences stem from their distinct steady-state current profiles.
elements.sent-2411-407: Altogether, the complementarity between CaL and Kir channels provides a robust mechanism for central sensitization in the dorsal horn.
elements.sent-2411-408: Neuronal bistability underlies short-term memory in neurons associated with sleep rhythms, motor control, working memory, epilepsy, schizophrenia, and pain amplification.
elements.sent-2411-409: However, this phenomenon requires the involvement of additional channels, such as voltage-gated potassium channels, to manifest within physiological ranges.
elements.sent-2411-410: In this context, a trade-off exists between bistability and the plausibility of resting potentials, making it unclear how robust bistability occurs under physiological conditions.
elements.sent-2411-411: Using a conductance-based model, we explored how M-type or inward rectifier potassium channels, in combination with L-type calcium channels, influence bistability.
elements.sent-2411-412: This study demonstrates that the type of bistability observed, as well as the presence of the aforementioned trade-off, can be explained by the steady-state shapes of the ionic currents flowing through the neuronal membrane around the spike threshold.
elements.sent-2411-413: We show that both inward rectifier potassium channels and L-type calcium channels promote bistability and plateau potentials, whereas M-type potassium channels tend to oppose them.
elements.sent-2411-414: As a result of changes in neuronal excitability, the bistability observed between resting and spiking exhibits distinct features and robustness that may influence neuronal processing.
elements.sent-2411-415: Nociceptive pain is a fundamental physiological sensation that protects the body integrity against noxious stimuli to avoid tissue damage \cite{woolf_what_2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-416: In the event of a significant increase in threat intensity, sensitization triggers functional changes within the nociceptive system \cite{latremoliere_central_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-417: A subset of mechanisms contributing to sensitization target projection neurons, rendering them hyperexcitable and thus amplifying nociception \cite{reali_integrated_2005,monteiro_switching-and_2006,zain_alterations_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-418: More specifically, projection neurons can switch from tonic firing to plateau potentials with sustained afterdischarges \cite{derjean_dynamic_2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-419: These afterdischarges are a sign of hyperexcitability of fundamental importance because they represent a substantial flow of information sent to supraspinal centers, triggering hyperalgesia and indicating a profound change in the sensory signal transmission properties \cite{cata_altered_2006,monteiro_switching-and_2006,robinson_altered_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-420: Indeed, afterdischarges extend the response to noxious stimuli by sustaining spiking following its end rather than reverting to a resting state---a feature commonly designated by bistability \cite{monteiro_switching-and_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-421: Neuronal bistability has been the subject of investigation in several different neuronal populations \cite{lee_bistability_1998,kazantsev_bistability_2011,dovzhenok_exploring_2012,engbers_bistability_2013,franci_balance_2013,borges_roles_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-422: Computational studies revealed that bistability between a resting and a spiking state may arise with a targeted increase in voltage-gated calcium channel conductance \cite{crunelli_window_2005,le_bistable_2006,borges_roles_2023 , but this must be combined with voltage-gated potassium channels to compensate for the induced hyperpolarization of resting potentials.}
elements.sent-2411-423: However, classical voltage-gated potassium channels also have the side effect of significantly reducing the level of bistability \cite{yuen_bistability_1995,franci_balance_2013,naudin_general_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-424: This leads to a trade-off between bistability robustness and resting potential adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-425: Conversely, an increasing body of work showed that the inward rectifier potassium (Kir) channels, a particular type of voltage-gated potassium channels can induce bistability \cite{shoemaker_neural_2011,sanders_nmda_2013,amarillo_inward_2018,delmoe_conditions_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-426: Evidence indicated that Kir channels are also present in superficial and deep projection neurons in the dorsal horn \cite{derjean_dynamic_2003,murata_neuronal_2016,ford_inward-rectifying_2016,malcangio_gabab_2018,brewer_enhanced_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-427: For this purpose, we used a conductance-based model to determine how these two pairs of ion channels affect bistability and reveal the mechanisms involved in each case.
elements.sent-2411-428: Additionally, our results indicated that these pairs of voltage-gated ion channels give rise to two types of bistability that differ in robustness to intrinsic variability and noisy input currents.
elements.sent-2411-429: Then, we point out the relevance of a combination of CaL and Kir channels for bistability by quantifying their robustness to noisy input currents and intrinsic variability using both types of potassium channels (KM or Kir) in combination with CaL channels.
elements.sent-2411-430: However, common voltage-gated potassium channels do not solely redress those equilibria, but weaken bistability.
elements.sent-2411-431: In this section, we illustrate this problem, first by adding L-type calcium (CaL) channels to a conductance-based model, creating unphysiological bistability, and then by adding M-type potassium (KM) channels, redressing resting potentials at the cost of weakening bistability.

elements.sent-2411-574: This method consists of computing the evolution of the membrane potential $V$ in response to a given applied current $I$ as: \begin{linenomath \begin{eqnarray}C \frac{dV}{dt} = - I_\mathrm{leak} - \sum_{\mathrm{ion} \in \mathcal{I}} I_{\mathrm{ion}} + I , \label{eq:dV}\end{eqnarray} \end{linenomath where $C$ is the membrane capacitance, $ I_{\mathrm{leak }$ is the leak current, $I_{\mathrm{ion }$ are the intrinsic ionic currents with $\mathcal{I}$ the set of all ionic channels.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-575: In this work, the ionic currents flowing in each type of voltage-dependent ion channels are defined by: \begin{linenomath \begin{eqnarray}I_{\mathrm{ion}} = \bar{g}_\mathrm{ion} \, m_{\mathrm{ion}}^{q_{\mathrm{ion}}} \, h_{\mathrm{ion}}^{r_{\mathrm{ion}}} \, (V-E_{\mathrm{ion}}) ,\end{eqnarray} \end{linenomath where $\bar{g _{\mathrm{ion }$ denotes the ion channels maximal conductance, $m_{\mathrm{ion }$ and $h_{\mathrm{ion }$ correspond to the voltage-dependent activation and inactivation gates of the ion channels, $q_{\mathrm{ion }$ is an integer between $1$ and $4$, $r_{\mathrm{ion }$ is an integer between $0$ and $1$, and $E_{\mathrm{ion }$ denotes the Nernst potential of the considered ionic current.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-576: The L-type calcium channels, embedding a calcium-dependency, were modeled using a Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz formalism \cite{hodgkin_effect_1949 such that: \begin{linenomath \begin{eqnarray}I_{\mathrm{CaL}} = \bar{p}_\mathrm{CaL} \, m_{\mathrm{CaL}}^{2} \, h_{\mathrm{CaL}}\mathrm{GHK}(V,\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \right]_i),\end{eqnarray} \end{linenomath with \begin{linenomath \begin{eqnarray}\mathrm{GHK}(V,\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \right]_i) = 10^{-3} \cdot 2F \cdot \left( \left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \right]_i \cdot \dfrac{-w}{e^{-w} -1} - \left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \right]_o \cdot \dfrac{w}{e^{w} -1}\right) ,\end{eqnarray} \end{linenomath and \begin{linenomath \begin{eqnarray}w = 10^{-3}\cdot V\cdot \dfrac{2F}{RT},\end{eqnarray} \end{linenomath where $F$ is the Faraday constant, $R$ is the perfect gas constant, $T$ is the temperature, $\left[\mathrm{Ca ^{2+ \right]_i $ is the intracellular calcium concentration, and $\left[\mathrm{Ca ^{2+ \right]_o $ is the extracellular calcium concentration, considered constant.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-577: The evolution of the intracellular calcium concentration was modeled as: \begin{linenomath \begin{eqnarray}\dfrac{d\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \right]_i}{dt} = - 10^7 \cdot \dfrac{I_{\mathrm{CaL}}}{10^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2Fd} + \dfrac{\left(\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \right]_{i,0}-\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \right]_i \right) }{\tau_\mathrm{Ca}},\end{eqnarray} \end{linenomath where $I_{\mathrm{CaL }$ is the intrinsic ionic current flowing in the L-type calcium channels $F$ is the Faraday constant, $d$ is the depth of the shell beneath the membrane, $\left[\mathrm{Ca ^{2+ \right]_{i,0}$ is the initial intracellular calcium concentration, and $\tau_\mathrm{Ca}$ is the buffering time constant.}}}}}
